skill.meta_description: Automatically solves Moltbook math captchas by parsing English text, extracting operations, calculating results, and formatting answers with two decimals.

security_scan.summary: The skill's code mostly matches its stated purpose (solving Moltbook math challenges and submitting answers), but it accesses a user credential file and a network API without declaring those requirements and the SKILL.md and code disagree about where account context is loaded—this inconsistency is concerning.

sections.Purpose & Capability: The name and files indicate a solver for Moltbook verification challenges, which aligns with the implementation: solveChallenge parses English-number words and verify() posts an answer to Moltbook's API. That capability is plausible for the stated purpose.
sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md claims the skill depends on memory/moltbook-state.json for account context and documents a /molt verify command, but the TypeScript code reads ~/.config/moltbook/
sections.Credentials: .json (CRED_PATH) and performs a network POST to https://www.moltbook.com/api/v1/verify. The SKILL.md does not disclose reading a credentials file or making network calls; those are runtime actions that should be declared and justified.
sections.Install Mechanism: No install spec — the package is instruction/code-only. That is low-risk in the sense that nothing external is downloaded at install time. The included code will run when invoked by the agent. ! CREDENTIALS The skill does not declare any required environment variables or config paths, yet the code reads the user's home config file (~/.config/moltbook/credentials.json) and expects an api_key inside. SKILL.md instead references memory/moltbook-state.json — this mismatch means the skill will access sensitive local credentials without declaring or justifying that access.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: always is false and the skill is user-invocable; autonomous invocation is allowed (platform default). While not privileged by manifest flags, the ability to read local credentials and call an external API increases its effective privilege and blast radius if invoked autonomously.
sections.Assessment: This skill appears to do what it claims (solve Moltbook math captchas and call Moltbook's API), but it reads a credentials file from ~/.config/moltbook/credentials.json and sends a POST to https://www.moltbook.com/api/v1/verify — neither of these are declared in SKILL.md or the skill metadata, and SKILL.md even claims a different dependency (memory/moltbook-state.json). Before installing or enabling this skill, consider: 1) Ask the developer to update SKILL.md and the manifest to explicitly declare the config path and required credential (what field name is expected in the file). 2) Inspect the credentials file and confirm it only contains the minimal API key the skill needs (avoid storing other secrets there). 3) If you don't trust the skill, do not provide your Moltbook API key; run it in an isolated/test account or environment first. 4) If you need stricter control, disable autonomous invocation for the agent or require manual confirmation before verification calls. 5) If you want to proceed, request the developer change the skill to read from the declared memory/moltbook-state.json or to accept the key via an explicitly-declared env/config interface so permission and auditing are clear.

skill.title: stream-formatter — ClawHub
skill.description: LLM streaming output formatter with auto buffer, format correction, sentence break optimization, markdown rendering, improve chat UX
skill.meta_description: LLM streaming output formatter with auto buffer, format correction, sentence break optimization, markdown rendering, improve chat UX

security_scan.summary: The skill's code and instructions match its stated purpose (streaming output formatting) and request no extra secrets or installs; the only notable external dependency is a pinned zod import from deno.land which is proportionate to the implementation.

sections.Purpose & Capability: Name/description (streaming formatter for LLM output) align with the provided SKILL.md, README, and index.ts implementation. All declared capabilities (buffering, markdown fixes, sentence break logic, deduplication) are implemented in the code. No unrelated
sections.Credentials: , binaries, or config paths are requested.
sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md instructs the agent to call the skill with actions init/process/reset and to supply streaming chunks from llm.streamResponse. The instructions do not ask the agent to read files, environment variables, or system state outside the scope of streaming text processing. There is no guidance to transmit data to external endpoints beyond returning the formatted output.
sections.Install Mechanism: There is no explicit install spec. The code imports zod from a pinned deno.land URL (https://deno.land/x/zod@v3.22.4). This is a common, traceable dependency host and the version is pinned; it does cause a runtime fetch of third-party code when executed. This is expected for a Deno/TypeScript skill but is worth noting since it pulls code at runtime. ✓ CREDENTIALS The skill declares no required environment variables, credentials, or config paths and the code does not read process.env or other secrets. The requested privileges are minimal and proportional to its stated purpose.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: always is false and the skill does not request persistent system changes, modify other skills, or write to global agent settings. Its state is limited to in-memory buffers (buffer, lastOutput, config).
sections.Assessment: This skill appears coherent and low-risk: it only formats streaming text, uses an in-memory buffer, and requires no secrets. Two practical checks before installing: (1) confirm your runtime environment is comfortable fetching a pinned module from deno.land (the skill imports zod@v3.22.4) — if your policy forbids remote imports, request a vendored/local dependency instead; (2) test the skill with non-sensitive text to validate behavior (deduplication and buffer handling) before using it with production data. If you need higher assurance, you can audit or vendor the zod dependency or run the code in a sandboxed environment.

skill.title: notion-clipper-skill — ClawHub
skill.description: Clip web pages to Notion. Fetches any URL via Chrome CDP, converts HTML to Markdown, then to Notion blocks, and saves to user-specified Notion database or page. Use when user wants to save/clip a webpage to Notion, or mentions "clip to notion", "save page to notion", "网页剪藏到Notion".
skill.meta_description: Clip web pages to Notion. Fetches any URL via Chrome CDP, converts HTML to Markdown, then to Notion blocks, and saves to user-specified Notion database or page. Use when user wants to save/clip a web…

security_scan.summary: The skill's code, instructions, and requirements are coherent with its stated purpose (launch Chrome via CDP, render a page, convert to Markdown and push to Notion); no obvious misdirection or hidden endpoints were found, but it does auto-install npm dependencies and writes browser/profile data to disk so take usual precautions.

sections.Purpose & Capability: The skill name/description match the implementation: it launches a local Chrome instance via CDP, renders and extracts page HTML, converts HTML→Markdown→Notion blocks, and calls the Notion API. Required artifacts (Chrome, a Notion integration key) and reading a local API key file are consistent with the stated functionality.
sections.Instruction Scope: The SKILL.md tells the agent to run npm install (if node_modules missing) and then run the TypeScript entry with npx tsx. That is within scope for a code-based clipper but grants the agent permission to install and execute dependencies at runtime. The instructions also ask the user to store the Notion API key at ~/.config/notion/api_key and to unset proxy env vars — both are consistent with operation, though the key is stored in plaintext on disk.
sections.Install Mechanism: There is no formal install spec; instead the runtime instructions auto-run `npm install` in the skill's scripts folder and use `npx -y tsx` at runtime. That causes packages to be fetched from the public npm registry (package-lock is included which helps reproducibility), and some dependencies (e.g., esbuild) have install scripts/binary downloads. This is expected for a Node-based tool but increases risk compared to a pure instruction-only skill because remote code and native binaries can be downloaded and executed.
sections.Credentials: The only credential the skill needs is the Notion integration API key (the code reads a file at a path resolved by paths.ts). It optionally respects NOTION_CLIPPER_CHROME_PATH to locate Chrome. There are no unrelated secrets requested. Storing the API key as a plaintext local file is functional but carries the usual risk if other processes can read that file.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: always:false and the skill is user-invocable (normal). The implementation creates/writes data: node_modules in the skill directory (from npm install) and a Chrome user-data-dir profile (a per-skill profile directory) for the spawned browser. That means cookies, local storage, or logged-in sessions could persist in that profile between runs — expected for a clipper but something users should be aware of.
sections.Assessment: This skill appears to do what it says, but before installing or running it consider: - The SKILL.md runs `npm install` and `npx -y tsx` at runtime. That will download and install packages (and native installer scripts) from npm. Review scripts/package.json and package-lock.json if you want reproducibility, or run in a sandbox/container. - The skill reads your Notion integration key from a plaintext file (~/.config/notion/api_key). Use a dedicated, limited-scope Notion integration and be prepared to revoke the key if you stop trusting the skill. - The tool creates a Chrome user-data-dir for its headful browsing; cookies and logged-in sessions can be stored there and persist between runs. If you need ephemeral behavior, remove the profile dir after use or run inside an isolated environment. - If you want extra caution: inspect the package.json and the top-level files, run `npm ci` yourself in an isolated environment, or run the script in a disposable VM/container. If anything looks unexpected, do not provide your real Notion API key.

versions.version_changelog_text:
## 1.65.0 - 2026-03-13

### Features
- `baoyu-post-to-wechat`: add placeholder image upload support with deduplication for markdown-embedded images

### Fixes
- `baoyu-post-to-wechat`: fix frontmatter parsing to allow leading whitespace and optional trailing newline

### Refactor
- `baoyu-post-to-wechat`: replace `renderMarkdownToHtml` with `renderMarkdownWithPlaceholders` for structured output

## 1.60.0 - 2026-03-11

### Features
- `baoyu-url-to-markdown`: support reusing existing Chrome CDP instances and fix port detection order

### Fixes
- `baoyu-post-to-x`: add missing `fs` import in x-article

### Refactor
- Unify all CDP skills to use shared `baoyu-chrome-cdp` package with vendored copies
- Simplify CLAUDE.md, move detailed documentation to `docs/` directory
- Publish skills directly from synced vendor, removing separate artifact preparation step

baoyu-post-to-wechat 1.0.0 – Initial Release

- Post content to WeChat Official Account via API or Chrome CDP, supporting both article and image-text (formerly 图文) formats.
- Handles HTML, markdown, and plain text; markdown inputs auto-convert external links into WeChat-friendly footnotes.
- Loads and prioritizes publishing preferences from EXTEND.md at project, XDG, or user level.
- Offers environment readiness checking and detailed troubleshooting guidance.
- Supports custom themes, color schemes, and author metadata via configuration.
- Includes publishing workflow and command-line usage examples for quick start.

skill.title: HostGuard — ClawHub
skill.description: Check whether OpenClaw is listening beyond localhost or running with elevated privileges, then offer a conservative lockdown fix.
skill.meta_description: Check whether OpenClaw is listening beyond localhost or running with elevated privileges, then offer a conservative lockdown fix.

security_scan.summary: The skill's code, declared requirements, and runtime instructions are coherent with its stated purpose: it inspects local env files, checks local listeners using lsof/netstat, determines elevation, and can back up-and-fix an env host entry on request.

sections.Purpose & Capability: Name and description (check listener binding + elevated privileges, offer conservative fix) match what the code and SKILL.md do. Declared requirements (whoami plus lsof or netstat) are consistent with inspecting privileges and network listeners. Declared config paths (.env.*) match the files the code reads.
sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md instructs only to read specific env files, run lsof/netstat to inspect local listeners, and check elevation; the code follows that. The skill does not attempt to read unrelated system config, request external network endpoints, or exfiltrate data. The fix behavior is constrained to editing an existing HOST/OPENCLAW_HOST entry and creating a .bak backup, as documented.
sections.Install Mechanism: No install spec (instruction-only with a code file). The implementation uses local system binaries (lsof/netstat/whoami) and Node APIs; there are no downloads or external package fetches in the provided code.
sections.Credentials: The skill requests no secrets or environment variables. It reads only the declared env files (.env variants). The required binaries and file paths are proportional to the stated purpose.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: The skill is not forced-always, does not claim permanent presence, and will only modify files when the explicit apply_lockdown_fix method is called. File modifications are limited to creating a .bak and editing the host entry in the specific env file (it will not add a host entry automatically).
sections.Assessment: This skill reads your local .env files, runs local network/listener checks (lsof or netstat) and a user-identity check (whoami or process.getuid) to assess exposure and privileges. It will only edit a file if you explicitly invoke the lockdown fix; when it does, it creates a .bak backup and only replaces an existing HOST/OPENCLAW_HOST value with 127.0.0.1. Before installing, ensure you are comfortable allowing the agent to run local commands and read the listed .env files; review any proposed change before confirming the fix. If you need tighter control, run the skill manually rather than allowing autonomous invocation.

Initial release of ClawGuard, a security assistant for OpenClaw.

- Checks if the OpenClaw service is listening beyond localhost and/or running with elevated privileges.
- Determines host and port configuration from prioritized environment files.
- Detects current network bindings and classifies listener state conservatively.
- Warns about elevated privilege use and possible network exposure without over-claiming risk.
- Offers a lockdown fix to restrict the listener to localhost, only when appropriate and with user consent.

skill.title: lead-generating — ClawHub
skill.description: Automate lead capture and tracking with Supabase storage and Make.com email workflows, managing conversations from new to qualified status.
skill.meta_description: Automate lead capture and tracking with Supabase storage and Make.com email workflows, managing conversations from new to qualified status.

security_scan.summary: The skill implementation matches its stated lead-capture purpose but it explicitly instructs the operator to supply a Supabase Service Role key (full-access credential), which is over‑privileged for an agent integration and undermines RLS protections — this mismatch warrants caution before install.

sections.Purpose & Capability: Name, README, SKILL.md, skill.json and the TypeScript code all implement lead capture, conversation logging, status updates and automation checks against a Supabase backend. The claimed capabilities align with the code and schemas.
sections.Instruction Scope: Runtime instructions ask operators to use the Supabase Service Role Key (SKILL.md / README) which grants full DB privileges and bypasses row-level security — broader scope than a typical per-org agent integration needs. Otherwise instructions stay within lead/automation scope and do not request arbitrary file or system reads.
sections.Install Mechanism: This is an instruction-only skill with a code file and package.json. There is no download-from-arbitrary-URL or installer; standard npm dependency (@supabase/supabase-js) is declared. No extract-from-URL installs or suspicious binaries were found.
sections.Credentials: The skill requires Supabase connection info (supabaseUrl, supabaseKey, orgId) in its config. The SKILL.md and README explicitly instruct using the Service Role Key rather than an anon key — that key is a full-access secret (SECRET-like) and is disproportionate compared to the function (which could be implemented with anon key + RLS or a limited server-side token).
sections.Persistence & Privilege: The skill is not always-enabled, does not request system-wide config changes, and its hooks are simple echo statements. It does not declare elevated platform privileges beyond normal runtime execution.
sections.Assessment: This skill appears to do what it says (lead capture, storing conversations, triggering automations) but it asks you to provide a Supabase Service Role Key — a full-access credential that bypasses Supabase row-level security and can read/write any table. Before installing, consider: - Avoid giving the agent the Service Role Key unless the skill runs in a trusted, server-side environment you control. Prefer using an Anon/public key with properly configured RLS policies, or a narrowly scoped server-side token that only allows the required inserts/queries. - Ask the skill author why the Service Role Key is required and whether the skill can work with RLS or a restricted API key. - Verify where the key will be stored by your platform (is it encrypted/secret-scoped?) and whether the agent runtime could leak it to logs or remote endpoints. - Review and/or run the code in a staging environment to confirm the skill only accesses the declared Supabase tables and does not call unexpected external endpoints. There are references to Make.com/Resend in docs, but no hardcoded webhook URLs in the code — you will need to configure those automations yourself. - Rotate the key after testing and monitor Supabase logs for unexpected queries. If you cannot host the skill in a trusted server-side environment or cannot supply a narrowly scoped credential, treat this skill as unsafe to run with a Service Role Key.

security_scan.summary: The ClawTrust skill is internally consistent with its stated purpose (an on‑chain identity/reputation/escrow SDK that talks to clawtrust.org), but there are a few small implementation notes you should understand before installing.

sections.Purpose & Capability: The name/description (on‑chain identity, reputation, USDC escrow, ERC‑8183 commerce) matches the SDK and documentation. The package requires no environment
sections.Credentials: and all network calls in the docs are routed to clawtrust.org, which is proportionate for a centralized trust service. Minor inconsistency: chain config objects include RPC URLs and contract addresses (sepolia.base.org, skalenodes, etc.), even though the SKILL.md and changelog repeatedly assert that agents should not call RPCs directly and that cross‑chain operations are routed server‑side. This is plausible (configs are useful for display/validation), but you should verify that none of the shipped helper utilities will invoke those RPCs from your agent runtime unless you explicitly opt in.
sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md instructions are focused on registration, heartbeats, gig lifecycle, reputation queries, webhooks, skill discovery and domain/contract metadata. The runtime guidance does not ask the agent to read unrelated local files or environment secrets. It does instruct agents to send/retain an x-agent-id (tempAgentId) and to configure webhooks and MCP endpoints (discovery metadata). Those behaviors are expected for this service but worth noting: webhooks cause clawtrust.org to POST to your URL, and registering an MCP endpoint will store its URL in the platform's discovery metadata.
sections.Install Mechanism: There is no install spec (instruction-only), which is low risk. The repository includes a full TypeScript SDK (source files, package.json) intended for Node.js >=18 usage; there is no automated installer provided. If you run the SDK locally, you will need Node and may build/execute the TypeScript — review and build steps are your responsibility. No remote download URLs or archive extraction are present in the skill metadata. ✓ CREDENTIALS The skill declares no required environment variables or credentials, which aligns with the documentation. It uses two auth models: x-agent-id for autonomous agents and SIWE headers for wallet‑protected endpoints; those are appropriate for the described functionality. One operational note: the platform issues/returns a circleWalletId and documents that Circle wallets are server‑managed (developer‑controlled wallets). That is coherent for a centralized escrow service but is an important trust decision (the platform controls the on‑chain wallet used for escrow operations).
sections.Persistence & Privilege: The skill is not always-included and uses normal autonomous invocation. It does not request to modify other skills or system-wide agent settings in the provided instructions. The SDK includes methods to set webhooks and to send periodic heartbeats — expected behavior, not elevated privilege.
sections.Assessment: This skill appears to do what it says: a TypeScript SDK and docs that interact only with https://clawtrust.org/api for identity, reputation, gigs, and escrow. Before installing: - Trust the platform: ClawTrust manages Circle USDC wallets server‑side (circleWalletId). If you plan to fund or receive USDC via this platform, understand that those wallets are controlled by ClawTrust and that custody/escrow behavior depends on their server‑side logic and audits. - Review the shipped code before executing it in your agent runtime. The SDK includes chain RPC URLs and contract addresses (harmless for display), but you should inspect utils/reputationSync.ts and the rest of the client to confirm no direct RPC or unexpected external calls will be made from your agent unless you intentionally configure that. - Be deliberate with webhooks and MCP endpoints: setting a webhook lets clawtrust.org POST events to a URL you provide; registering an MCP endpoint publishes that endpoint in discovery lists (the skill says the platform will not call MCP endpoints, but other agents may). Only expose webhook/MCP endpoints you control and trust. - If you will use wallet flows (SIWE/fromWallet), ensure signing actions are performed only when you intend to perform on‑chain or wallet‑sensitive operations — the SDK's fromWallet requires a wallet provider and reads eth_chainId locally. If you want higher assurance, ask the maintainer for an independent security/audit report for the ClawTrust server and the deployed contracts, and audit the reputationSync implementation to confirm it truly proxies cross‑chain operations through clawtrust.org rather than performing RPC calls directly.

skill.title: crypto-treasury-ops — ClawHub
skill.description: Safely manage EVM treasury operations and native Hyperliquid trading for OpenClaw agents, including wallet balance checks, guarded token transfers, cross-cha...
skill.meta_description: Safely manage EVM treasury operations and native Hyperliquid trading for OpenClaw agents, including wallet balance checks, guarded token transfers, cross-cha...

security_scan.summary: The skill's code and instructions implement guarded crypto treasury operations (which legitimately require a private key and API keys), but the package metadata/registry declarations are inconsistent (no required env vars listed, and the skill is described as instruction-only despite many source files), increasing risk and warranting extra review before use.

sections.Purpose & Capability: The skill's stated purpose (treasury ops + Hyperliquid trading) matches the code: it reads balances, signs EVM transactions, bridges, and interacts with Hyperliquid and 0x/Li.Fi. However the registry metadata claims 'Required env vars: none' and 'No install spec — instruction-only', while SKILL.md and the source clearly require TREASURY_PRIVATE_KEY and ZEROX_API_KEY and include full TypeScript code. That metadata vs. implementation mismatch is incoherent and could trick an installer into not providing proper safeguards.
sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md and the source are consistent about runtime behavior: reading env vars, building, and running a CLI that returns structured JSON. The runtime contract documents which tools require the private key and which are read-only. The instructions do not instruct the agent to read unrelated system files or exfiltrate secrets; operations call expected external endpoints (0x, LI.FI, Hyperliquid, Solana RPCs).
sections.Install Mechanism: There is no install script in the registry spec (low install-surface), but the package contains a full Node project with dependencies (npm packages: @nktkas/hyperliquid, @solana/web3.js, viem, dotenv, zod). That's a typical pattern for a Node-based skill, but the absence of an install spec combined with many source files is inconsistent. Dependencies are standard for this domain (moderate trust surface since npm packages are third-party).
sections.Credentials: The skill requires highly sensitive secrets (TREASURY_PRIVATE_KEY for signing transactions and ZEROX_API_KEY for swap quotes). Those credentials are proportional to the claimed functionality. The concern is the manifest omission: registry metadata lists no required env vars while SKILL.md explicitly requires them. Also many optional environment variables (RPC overrides, bridge/swap API keys, safety controls) are available; ensure only minimal required secrets are provided and prefer delegated signers/KMS.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: always:false and model invocation enabled (default) — normal for a skill. The skill writes JSONL audit logs to .runtime/treasury-ops.log (documented). It does not appear to modify other skills or system-wide agent configuration. Note: allowing autonomous invocation while the agent has access to a live treasury private key increases the blast radius if the agent is allowed to call the skill without strict guardrails.
sections.Assessment: This package is functionally coherent for guarded crypto treasury tasks, but there are important red flags you should address before installing: - Metadata inconsistency: The registry metadata claims no required env vars and 'instruction-only', but the SKILL.md and source require TREASURY_PRIVATE_KEY and ZEROX_API_KEY and include a full Node project. Treat the metadata as unreliable and assume the skill requires the private key. - Private key handling: Do NOT store a live treasury private key in plaintext .env on a host where the agent or other processes can access it. Use a vault, KMS, HSM, or a delegated signer that exposes signing-only RPC (or hardware signer) wherever possible. - Autonomous invocation risk: Because the skill can be invoked by the agent automatically, giving the agent access to TREASURY_PRIVATE_KEY allows it to initiate on-chain transfers. If you must use this skill with an LLM agent, either disable autonomous invocation for the agent or ensure strict allowlists and confirmation thresholds (TREASURY_ALLOWLIST, STRICT_ALLOWLIST, confirmation thresholds) are configured and enforced. - Audit the code & dependencies: Review the included source (you have it) for any network calls or unexpected behavior; verify npm dependencies are acceptable and consider an offline build from source rather than pulling remote releases. Verify the Hyperliquid and LI.FI endpoints are correct and intended. - Run in isolation & test with dryRun: Start with read-only operations and safe dry runs; use the skill's dryRun defaults (and safety_check / quote_operation) before any state-changing command. Ensure logging destination (.runtime/treasury-ops.log) is writable only by a controlled user and that logs do not contain raw private keys. - Ask the publisher for clarification: Request corrected registry metadata that lists required env vars and clarifies install instructions. The metadata mismatch reduces trust — get confirmation of the package provenance and intended deployment process. If you cannot verify provenance or cannot adopt delegated signing, treat this skill as risky for production treasury access.

Required and optional env vars (PRIVATE_KEY, BSC_RPC_URL, CREATION_FEE_WEI, 8004_NFT_ADDRESS, TAX_* etc.) are relevant to on-chain operations and token creation/trading.
sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md explicitly requires presenting a User Agreement and Security Notice and forbids running private-key-using operations until the user consents. Note: the consent requirement is an agent-level policy (text in SKILL.md) — the shipped scripts themselves do not enforce user consent, so the agent/platform must honor that policy to avoid accidental use of the PRIVATE_KEY.
sections.Install Mechanism: There is no high-risk remote install spec embedded in the skill bundle. SKILL.md recommends installing the published npm package @four-meme/four-meme-ai@latest (a normal distribution channel for a CLI). The repository also includes full source files — no anonymous HTTP downloads or extract-from-arbitrary-URL installs are present in the manifest.
sections.Credentials: The only required credential is PRIVATE_KEY (declared as primaryEnv) which is expected for on-chain writes (create/buy/sell/send/register). Optional env vars are related to RPC URL, creation fees, NFT address, and tax/token config — all relevant to token creation/trading. No unrelated secrets or multiple unrelated credentials are requested.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: The skill is not set to always:true. It can be invoked autonomously (platform default) but there is no sign it modifies other skills or requests persistent elevated privileges. The main risk stems from the PRIVATE_KEY use (normal for blockchain tools) rather than any platform privilege escalation.
sections.Assessment: This skill appears coherent for interacting with Four.Meme on BSC, but it requires your PRIVATE_KEY to perform on-chain operations — that is high-risk. Before installing or using it: (1) only provide a PRIVATE_KEY for a throwaway/trading wallet with minimal funds; never paste your main seed phrase or private key into chat; (2) review the npm package (@four-meme/four-meme-ai) and its source before global install; (3) when asked to approve any action, double-check the transaction details (recipient, amounts); (4) prefer using hardware wallets or offline signing where possible (the scripts expect a raw private key and will sign/send transactions); (5) ensure the agent/platform enforces the SKILL.md consent step (do not let an automated agent run create/buy/sell/send/8004-register without explicit, interactive user consent). versions.version_changelog_text:
Version 1.0.9

- Fixed the suspicious warnings
- No function changed

Minor update to environment variable metadata and version.

- Expanded metadata to explicitly declare new optional environment variables, including token creation and tax-related fields.
- Updated skill version in metadata to 1.0.5.
- No changes to functionality, CLI, or documentation content.

- Clarified environmental variable handling and setup instructions for secure skill operation.
- Improved instructions for .env usage and location for standalone CLI use.
- No functional or CLI command changes. Documentation and user guidance updates only.
- drop TAX env var usage from SKILL

- Added new script: scripts/create-token-instant.ts.
- Introduced a one-shot token creation command using fourmeme create-instant.
- Documentation updated to mention create-instant as a one-step alternative to the standard API + chain flow.
- No breaking changes to existing flows; new method is additive.

four-meme-ai v1.0.4

- Added new reference documentation: agent-creator-and-wallets.md in the references directory.
- No functional or behavioral changes to the skill code or logic.
- Improves developer/user guidance with expanded reference material.

- Clarified OpenClaw environment variable handling: only PRIVATE_KEY is required and injected; BSC_RPC_URL is now marked as optional.
- Updated documentation to specify that BSC_RPC_URL should be set in the global environment or project `.env` if needed.
- Improved guidance for environment variable configuration and enabling this skill within OpenClaw.
- No functional or CLI changes; documentation and metadata only.
- Support the CLI automatically loads `.env` from that current working directory.
- Modified Display Name

First release: Four.meme Agent Skill

Create token: Full API flow (nonce → login → upload image → create) and on-chain TokenManager2.createToken; supports normal and Tax tokens (tokenTaxInfo).
Query: Public config (config), on-chain token info (token-info), REST token list / detail / rankings (token-list, token-get, token-rankings).
Trading: Buy/sell quotes (quote-buy, quote-sell) and execution (buy, sell); TokenManager2 (V2) only, BSC.
Events: Fetch TokenManager2 events (TokenCreate, TokenPurchase, TokenSale, LiquidityAdded).
Tax token: On-chain fee/tax query (tax-info).
Send: Send BNB or ERC20 to an address (send).
EIP-8004: Identity NFT register (8004-register) and balance query (8004-balance).
Safety: User Agreement and Security Notice (bilingual); no private-key or on-chain write operations until the user agrees.
Docs: SKILL.md (CLI table and flows), references (API/contracts/errors), and official Protocol Integration link.
Environment: BSC only; requires PRIVATE_KEY (or FOURMEME_PRIVATE_KEY), optional BSC_RPC_URL. OpenClaw: configure via skills.entries["four-meme-ai"].

skill.title: Agent Task Queue — ClawHub
skill.description: Build and operate a multi-agent task queue in OpenClaw with priority queues, delayed/dead-letter queues, scheduling, retry/timeout control, dependency manage...
skill.meta_description: Build and operate a multi-agent task queue in OpenClaw with priority queues, delayed/dead-letter queues, scheduling, retry/timeout control, dependency manage...

security_scan.summary: The package implements a local TypeScript multi-agent task queue whose code, instructions, and dependency requests are coherent with the stated purpose.

sections.Purpose & Capability: The name/description (multi-agent task queue) matches the included TypeScript runtime (TaskQueue, Scheduler, DependencyManager, and storage backends). The package.json dependencies (redis, better-sqlite3) align with the provided Redis and SQLite storage implementations.
sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md instructs only queue-related actions (importing TaskQueue/Scheduler, picking storage backend, registering handlers, enqueueing tasks, running scheduler). It advises running npm install / npm run check; it does not instruct reading unrelated files, environment variables, or posting data to unknown endpoints.
sections.Install Mechanism: There is no automated install spec in the registry bundle; the README/SKILL.md expect you to run npm install locally. Dependencies are from npm and there are no downloads from arbitrary URLs. Note: better-sqlite3 is a native module that may require build tools when installed.
sections.Credentials: The skill declares no required env vars or credentials. Redis usage is opt-in via the RedisStorage constructor (accepts a URL argument) — no hidden env access is present. No unrelated secrets are requested.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: The skill is not forced-always, does not request elevated platform privileges, and does not modify other skills or global agent configuration. Running the SQLite backend will create a local DB file; using Redis will connect to a provided Redis endpoint.
sections.Assessment: This skill appears to be what it claims: a bundled TypeScript task-queue runtime. Before installing or running it: (1) inspect package.json and be prepared to run npm install (better-sqlite3 is a native module and may require build tools); (2) if you use RedisStorage, only point it at Redis instances you trust because task data and dependency results will be stored there; (3) SQLiteStorage will create a local file (task-queue.sqlite) in the working directory; (4) running npm test or npm run check will execute the test suite and build steps — run these in a sandbox if you are cautious; (5) note that task handlers you register execute arbitrary code and therefore can access network, files, or secrets available to the runtime; review any handlers you or the agent provides before allowing the scheduler to run continuously.

security_scan.summary: The skill's files, instructions, and required configuration are coherent with its stated purpose (integrating Maton OAuth/API into the Clawdbot Gateway UI) and do not request unrelated credentials or suspicious installs.

sections.Purpose & Capability: Name/description match the included backend and UI integration files. The skill only requires adding a Maton API key and copying UI/backend handlers into the Gateway — these are expected for a SaaS-OAuth integration.

Confirm you trust maton.ai and the ctrl.maton.ai endpoint, and consider scoping/rotating the Maton API key per best practices. If you want extra assurance, audit the referenced files in-place after copying (they are provided here) and test in a staging environment before enabling in production.

skill.title: XPR Code Sandbox — ClawHub
skill.description: Execute JavaScript code in a secure sandbox for data processing, computation, and quick expression evaluation without network or filesystem access.
skill.meta_description: Execute JavaScript code in a secure sandbox for data processing, computation, and quick expression evaluation without network or filesystem access.

security_scan.summary: The skill's code and runtime instructions are consistent with a JavaScript sandbox for computation and do not request unrelated credentials or installs, but provenance is unknown and sandbox-escape risks (common with vm-based sandboxes) warrant caution.

sections.Credentials: or installs, but provenance is unknown and sandbox-escape risks (common with vm-based sandboxes) warrant caution. Details ▾
sections.Purpose & Capability: Name, description, declared tools (execute_js, eval_expression) and the included src/index.ts align: the implementation uses Node's vm to run JS with explicit globals and blocked require/process/global access. No unrelated env vars, binaries, or installs are requested.
sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md limits scope to isolated JS execution and explicitly forbids network/filesystem/imports; the code enforces these restrictions by creating a limited context and undefining require/process/global. However, vm-based sandboxes can be subtle to get correct — the instructions do not permit reading system state or secrets, and the code does not appear to do so, but there remains a non-zero risk of sandbox escape vectors in complex JS environments.
sections.Install Mechanism: No install spec; skill is instruction/code-only and uses Node built-ins. Nothing in the manifest pulls external code or runs remote installers. ✓ CREDENTIALS The skill requires no environment variables, no credentials, and no config paths. The code does not access process.env or other outside credentials. Requested privileges are proportional to the stated purpose.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: always is false; the skill is user-invocable and allows autonomous invocation (platform default). It does not modify other skills or request permanent elevated presence.
sections.Assessment: This skill appears internally consistent for running JavaScript computations in a sandbox and does not ask for credentials or installs. Before installing, consider: (1) provenance — the source/homepage is unknown; prefer skills from known authors. (2) Treat it as untrusted when executing third-party code: test with non-sensitive inputs and small timeouts. (3) Although the code explicitly blocks require/process/global and disables code-generation-from-strings, vm-based sandboxes have historically had escape techniques — avoid sending secrets or sensitive data into the sandbox. (4) If you need stronger guarantees, run similar workloads in a dedicated, isolated execution environment (separate process/container) or seek a sandbox with formal audits.

skill.title: Agent Chat Nostr — ClawHub
skill.description: Command-line Nostr client for agent-to-agent encrypted messaging, identity login, and small file sharing using public Nostr relays.
skill.meta_description: Command-line Nostr client for agent-to-agent encrypted messaging, identity login, and small file sharing using public Nostr relays.

security_scan.summary: The skill mostly does what it claims (a Nostr CLI) but the package metadata and documentation omit that it stores your private key unencrypted on disk and the README claims file-sharing support that the CLI does not expose — these inconsistencies merit caution before installation.

sections.Purpose & Capability: Name/description match the code: it is a Nostr-based CLI that logs in with an nsec, sends/receives encrypted DMs, and listens on public relays. However the registry metadata lists no
sections.Credentials: /config paths, yet the code persistently stores the user's private key (nsec) in ~/.agent-chat/config.json. The SKILL.md claims small-file support but the CLI commands in src/index.ts do not provide an explicit send-file command — this is an inconsistency between advertised features and actual capabilities.
sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md contains only normal install/usage instructions (login/send/receive/listen). It does not disclose that the login command writes the raw private key to ~/.agent-chat/config.json. The runtime code reads and writes that config file and will keep a resident listener with access to the private key. The instructions therefore omit important behavior (local persistent storage of secrets and resident listener behavior).
sections.Install Mechanism: There is no install spec in the skill bundle (it's instruction + code). SKILL.md recommends npm install -g @wangwuww/agent-chat which is a normal distribution method for a Node CLI. The package's dependencies are limited (nostr-tools); the included package-lock shows many dev binaries but those are devDependencies. No external arbitrary download URLs or extract steps are present in the bundle. ! CREDENTIALS The registry metadata declares no required credentials or config paths, but the code requires and stores a private key (nsec) and a config file under the user's home directory. Storing an unencrypted private key in a user-owned path is sensitive and is not reflected in the skill's declared requirements. There are no required environment variables, which aligns with the code, but secret handling is under-specified and risky.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: The skill does not request always:true and does not modify other skills or system-wide agent settings. It does create a per-user config directory (~/.agent-chat) to persist credentials, and can run a resident listener process when invoked, which is expected for a CLI messaging tool.
sections.Assessment: Before installing: understand this CLI will ask you to provide your private key (nsec) and will save it unencrypted at ~/.agent-chat/config.json. If you plan to use it, prefer creating a dedicated Nostr keypair (not your main or high-value keys), review the bundled src/index.ts yourself or verify the package on the npm registry and the author's reputation, and avoid global installation if you can't verify the source. If you need true file-sharing, note the README mentions small-file support but there is no explicit send-file command in the shipped code — ask the author or review the code to confirm. Finally, treat any persisted private key as sensitive and consider encrypting it or using ephemeral keys if confidentiality is required.
